coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence/colorimetric sensor based on aptamers-molecular imprinted polymers synergistic recognition for ultrasensitive and interference-free detection of aflatoxin B1
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Xiaopeng
Wang, Ke
Yang, Yufan
Ding, Baomiao
Yu, Chunqi
coredata.dc:description: This work aimed to develop a fluorescence/colorimetric sensor for the ultrasensitive detection of AFB1 based on the aptamers and molecular imprinted polymers (MIPs). The polydopamine on Fe3O4 (Fe3O4@MIP) was used to achieve efficient separation of AFB1. The aptamer-modified carbon dots and metal-organic frameworks (Apt-CDs@MOF) were used to form sandwich particles (Fe3O4@MIP-AFB1-Apt-CDs@MOF). The sandwich particles were separated and removed using magnets, and the remaining Apt-CDs@MOF in suspension was used for both fluorescence and colorimetric detection. The sensor reached the linear range of 0.05–150 ng mL−1 (fluorescence channel) and 0.1–100 ng mL−1 (colorimetric channel). The detection limit can be as low as 37.0 pg mL−1 (fluorescence channel) and 13.0 pg mL−1 (colorimetric channel). Through the clever use of sandwich structure, the sensor represents a novel method for interference-free detection of AFB1. The proposed sensor effectiveness was further validated by quantifying AFB1 in untreated edible oil, which shows great potential for application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-mode detection
Dual recognition
CDs@MOF
Sandwich
AFB1

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analytical performance of the fluorescence/colorimetric strategy
Selectivity, repeatability, reproducibility and interference studies
Monitoring of AFB1 in edible oil samples

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Hubei Provincial Department of Education Science Research Program Young Talents Project

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We greatly appreciate the support from Hubei Provincial Department of Education Science Research Program Young Talents Project (Q20231304).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The high-resolution TEM of CDs@MOF. (B) XRD patterns of CDs@MOF. (C) XPS survey spectra of CDs@MOF. (D) XRD C 1 s spectra of CDs@MOF. (E) UV–vis absorption spectra of reaction system with different reagents (Inset: photographs of corresponding reaction system). (F) Fluorescence spectra of CDs@MOF under various excitation wavelengths.

(A) SEM image of Fe3O4@MIP-AFB1-Apt-CDs@MOF. (B) Zeta potential of different materials. UV–vis absorption spectra (C) and fluorescence spectra (D) of different reaction system in the presence of AFB1 (a: Apt-CDs@MOF; b: Fe3O4@NIP/AFB1/Apt-CDs@MOF; c: Fe3O4@MIP/AFB1/Apt-CDs@MOF). UV–vis absorption spectra (E) and fluorescence spectra (F) of different reaction system in the absence of AFB1 (a: Apt-CDs@MOF; b: Fe3O4@NIP/Apt-CDs@MOF; c: Fe3O4@MIP/Apt-CDs@MOF).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the fluorescence/colorimetric strategy at different AFB1 concentrations (0.05, 0.1, 1.0, 2.5, 25, 100 and 150 ng mL−1); (B) correlation between fluorescence intensity and logarithm of AFB1 concentration. (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of the fluorescence/colorimetric strategy at different AFB1 concentrations (0.1, 1.0, 10, 25, 50, 75 and 100 ng mL−1); (D) correlation between absorption values and AFB1 concentration (inset: solution colors).

(A) The selectivity of the dual-mode sensor towards AFB1 and the interferents (AFM1, AFG1, OTA and FB1). (B) Influence of interferents on the performance of the dual-mode sensor (AFB1, AFB1 + 20-fold AFM1, AFB1 + 20-fold AFG1, AFB1 + 20-fold OTA, AFB1 + 20-fold FB1). (C) Influence of ions on the performance of the dual-mode sensor (AFB1, AFB1 + 100-fold Hg2+, AFB1 + 100-fold Zn2+, AFB1 + 100-fold Mg2+, AFB1 + 100-fold Cu2+, AFB1 + 100-fold Na+, AFB1 + 100-fold Cl−, AFB1 + 100-fold SO4 2−). The repeatability (D) and reproducibility (E) of the dual-mode sensor. (F) Stability of the dual-mode sensing platform. Plots of absorption intensity (G) and fluorescence intensity (H) for the dual-mode sensor in in different interference substances containing 15 ng mL− 1 AFB1 (a: PBS, b: dilute peanut oil by 5 times, c: dilute peanut oil by 1 time, d: peanut oil).
(A) Schematic illustration of the sandwich structure construction process. (B) The detection mechanism of fluorescence/colorimetric sensors.
Comparison of different dual-mode methods for AFB1 detection.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence/colorimetric sensor based on aptamers-molecular imprinted polymers synergistic recognition for ultrasensitive and interference-free detection of aflatoxin B1

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science, Yangtze University, Jingzhou 434023, Hubei Province, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Science, Yangtze University, Jingzhou 434023, Hubei Province, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work aimed to develop a fluorescence/colorimetric sensor for the ultrasensitive detection of AFB1 based on the aptamers and molecular imprinted polymers (MIPs). The polydopamine on Fe3O4 (Fe3O4@MIP) was used to achieve efficient separation of AFB1. The aptamer-modified carbon dots and metal-organic frameworks (Apt-CDs@MOF) were used to form sandwich particles (Fe3O4@MIP-AFB1-Apt-CDs@MOF). The sandwich particles were separated and removed using magnets, and the remaining Apt-CDs@MOF in suspension was used for both fluorescence and colorimetric detection. The sensor reached the linear range of 0.05–150 ng mL−1 (fluorescence channel) and 0.1–100 ng mL−1 (colorimetric channel). The detection limit can be as low as 37.0 pg mL−1 (fluorescence channel) and 13.0 pg mL−1 (colorimetric channel). Through the clever use of sandwich structure, the sensor represents a novel method for interference-free detection of AFB1. The proposed sensor effectiveness was further validated by quantifying AFB1 in untreated edible oil, which shows great potential for application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Aflatoxin (AF), a dangerous mycotoxin in food, has been isolated and more than 20 types have been identified, including AFB1, AFM1, AFG1, etc. (Qian et al., 2020; Yang, Hui, et al., 2024). Among them, the determination of AFB1 is one of the important indicators in food safety assessment (Roushani et al., 2022). The entry of AFB1 into the food chain causes economic losses, and poses a threat to human health (Wang & Zhao, 2020). Continuous human exposure to AFB1, even at quite low concentrations, can lead to severe teratogenicity, carcinogenicity and nephrotoxicity (Chi & Liu, 2023; Tang et al., 2023). In response, some countries have imposed regulations on AFB1 levels in agricultural products and set the maximum residue level at 2 μg kg−1 (Chi & Liu, 2023). Therefore, the detection of AFB1 has become an urgent and central issue in the field of food safety.

Dual-mode readout analytical strategies can provide self-calibration for the measurement, and have greater flexibility under different detection conditions (Cui et al., 2023; Yan et al., 2023; Yang, Wang, et al., 2024). Fluorescence/colorimetric sensors with two readout modes are of great advantage in the field of food safety (Liu et al., 2023; Tang et al., 2022; Wu et al., 2022). The fluorescence detection mode has the advantages of fast response and high sensitivity (He et al., 2024). Colorimetry is an effective analytical method that is simple, low-cost and works with the naked eye (Termeau et al., 2023). Unfortunately, reports on fluorescence/colorimetric sensors are prone to interference and discoloration from complex sample matrices, resulting in lower sensitivity, selectivity and accuracy in practical applications (Yang, Wang, et al., 2024). Therefore, it is of great importance to develop an ultrasensitive and interference-free fluorescence/colorimetric sensor for the detection of AFB1.

Two crucial factors, the recognition element and the sensor structure, play a role in the construction of highly accurate and selective fluorescence/colorimetric sensors (Shen et al., 2024). Antibodies are used to construct sandwich biosensors, which have high specificity, accuracy, and sensitivity. However, sandwich biosensors based on double antibody recognition elements are not very suitable for the detection of small molecule that have only a few epitopes (Bai et al., 2021; Fei et al., 2023). As alternatives to antibodies, aptamers and molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have been successfully used in sandwich biosensors (Geng et al., 2024). In sandwich assays, MIPs and aptamers not only adopt their respective advantages, but also show high specificity in detection beyond a single recognition element (Chi & Liu, 2023). These sandwich biosensors based on dual recognition elements of MIPs and aptamers can also reduce the likelihood of nonspecific recognition of interfering substances in complex substrates (Geng et al., 2024). In view of the above considerations, the development of a fluorescence/colorimetric sensor based on synergistic recognition of MIPs and aptamers could enable the ultrasensitive and accurate detection of AFB1 in complex substrates.

The nanomaterials used to construct fluorescence/colorimetric sensors are also crucial for their performance. Carbon dots (CDs) are widely used due to their excellent peroxidase-like activity and optical properties (Liu et al., 2019). However, due to the easy aggregation of CDs, their properties can be severely degraded (Wang, Liu, et al., 2021). Therefore, it is necessary to introduce CDs into some hosts to disperse them. Metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) as optical and catalytic materials offer potential applications for the construction of sensing platforms (Yang, Wang, et al., 2024). Their large specific surface area make them ideal for loading CDs in the construction of sensors. The embedding of CDs in MOF (CDs@MOF) can prevent the aggregation of CDs, and the synergistic effect of CDs and MOF contributes to the composite materials exhibiting superior peroxidase-like activity and fluorescence property (Wang, Liu, et al., 2021). Based on the above-mentioned characteristics, the fluorescence property of CDs@MOF can be utilized for fluorescence detection, and the peroxidase-like activity of CDs@MOF can catalyze the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzydine (TMB) to blue oxidized TMB in the presence of H2O2.

In this work, a fluorescence/colorimetric sensor for ultrasensitive and interference-free detection of AFB1 was developed. Aptamers and MIPs were used to construct the synergistic sandwich detection strategy, and the composites of CDs@MOF served as both fluorescence probes and nanoenzymes to amplify the signals. The design principle and detection mechanism of the sensor are shown in Scheme 1 . When AFB1 was present in real sample, Fe3O4@MIPs captured and accumulated AFB1 in complex food matrices. Then the Fe3O4@MIPs were separated using a magnet and transferred to Apt-CDs@MOF suspension. Subsequently, Apt-CDs@MOF identified the AFB1 captured by Fe3O4@MIPs and formed the sandwich structure (Fe3O4@MIPs-AFB1-Apt-CDs@MOF). This was followed by magnetic separation in which Fe3O4@MIPs-AFB1-Apt-CDs@MOF was isolated. The higher the AFB1 concentration, the less Apt-CDs@MOF remained in the suspension. The fluorescence and colorimetric signal were diminished. With the clever use of the sandwich structure, the developed dual-mode sensor excluded most of interfering substances and was effectively used to detect AFB1 in untreated edible oil.

AFB1, AFM1, AFG1, fumonisin B1 (FB1), ochratoxin A (OTA), TMB, 2-methylimidazole, 5,7-dimethoxycoumarin, and dopamine were sourced from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-ethyl-3 - (3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), and ZnO were supplied from McLean Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). H2O2, FeCl3·6H2O, 2-methylimidazole, and sodium acetate (NaAc) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Deionized water (resistivity higher than 18.2 MΩ) was used to prepare all solutions. The aptamer was synthesized by Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), with the sequences listed as follows: 5’-NH2-GCACGTGTTGTCTCTCTGTGTCTCGTGC-3’.

Scanning electron micrographs (SEM) were taken with the Thermo Fisher Scientific Apreo 2S+ scanning electron microscope. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken using JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were collected using a Thermo Fisher X-ray photoelectron spectrometer, and X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using a Bruker diffractometer. Fluorescence spectra were measured with a Hitachi fluorescence spectrometer. UV–vis spectra were recorded using TU-1900 spectrophotometer.

First, Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized according to a previous report (Wang, Liang, et al., 2021). Then 5,7-dimethoxycoumarin and dopamine were used as dummy templates and functional monomers, respectively, and the Fe3O4@MIPs were prepared. In brief, Fe3O4 (100 mg), 10 mL 5,7-dimethoxycoumarin solution (5.0 mmol L−1) and dopamine (75 mg) sequentially added to 40 mL Tris–HCl buffer solution (10 mM, pH 8.5) under ultrasound. Then the mixture was shaken at 200 rpm for 4 h. The obtained product separated by the magnet was added to methanol-acetic acid eluent (9,1, v/v) under stirring to remove templates, and the products were denoted as Fe3O4@MIP. It is noteworthy that the synthesis of the magnetic non-imprinted polymers (Fe3O4@NIP) was the same as that of Fe3O4@MIP. The only difference was that no dummy templates were added.

CDs@MOF nanoparticles were prepared with some modifications according to a previous report (Wang, Liu, et al., 2021). In brief, ZnO (0.2 mg) and 2-methylimidazole (0.8 mg) were mixed and grinded uniformly. Then the mixture was transferred to a Teflon stainless steel reactor and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. Finally, the obtained solid was washed with ethanol for several times and dried at 60 °C for 3 h. The obtained products were named as CDs@MOF.

The AFB1 aptamers and CDs@MOF conjugates were obtained by covalent conjugation. NHS (200 μL, 0.25 M), EDC (200 μL, 1.0 M), and AFB1 aptamer (150 μL, 1.0 μM) were mixed, and the solution was shaken for 1.0 h at 37 °C. Then PBS (4.7 mL, pH 7.4, 0.01 M) and CDs@MOF (75 mg) were added to the above solution, and the mixture was shaken overnight at 37 °C. Finally, the product (Apt-CDs@MOF) was washed several times with PBS (pH 7.4, 0.01 M). Apt-CDs@MOF (50 mg) was dispersed in PBS (10 mL, pH 7.4, 0.01 M) and stored at 4 °C.

The Fe3O4@MIPs-AFB1-Apt-CDs@MOF complexes were prepared in the following steps. First, 5 mg Fe3O4@MIPs was added to PBS (2.0 mL, pH 8.0, 0.01 M) containing AFB1. The mixture was shaken gently for 25 min at room temperature. Then the mixture was collected using magnets and washed several times with deionized water, and then the resulting mixture (Fe3O4@MIPs-AFB1) was resuspended in PBS (2.0 mL, pH 7.4, 0.01 M). Subsequently, Apt-CDs@MOF suspension (5 mL, 5.0 mg mL−1) was added to the above-mentioned Fe3O4@MIPs-AFB1 suspension, and incubated for 40 min. Finally, the Fe3O4@MIPs-AFB1-Apt-CDs@MOF complexes were collected and separated, and the remaining Apt-CDs@MOF in suspension was used for the subsequent detection based on fluorescence or colorimetric methods.

In fluorescence mode, 1 mL of the above-mentioned suspension was added to 4 mL acetate buffer solution (ABS, pH 5.0, 0.2 M). The fluorescence spectra of the suspension were measured. The optimal excitation and maximum emission wavelengths were identified at 280 nm and 381 nm, respectively.

In colorimetric mode, 100 μL of the suspension containing Apt-CDs@MOF and 60. μL TMB (10 mM) were added to 1.8 mL ABS (pH 4.0, 0.2 M). Upon the addition of 40 μL H2O2 (100 mM), the suspension turned blue. The absorption intensity at 652 nm and photograph were recorded.

The morphologies of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@MIPs were characterized by using SEM (Fig. S1). As shown in Fig. S1A, the Fe3O4 nanoparticles are uniformly spherical with a particle size of about 250 nm. Fig. S1B shows the morphology of Fe3O4@MIPs. The spherical particles accumulate and the average particle size increases, indicating that the polydopamine was successfully deposited on the surface of Fe3O4.

To achieve the optimal recognition and adsorption performance of Fe3O4@MIPs, some key experimental parameters, including dopamine concentration, elution time, and adsorption time, were optimized using UV–vis spectroscopy (Fig. S2). The results show that the detection and adsorption performance of Fe3O4@MIPs is best when the dopamine concentration, elution time and adsorption time are 1.5 mg mL−1, 4 h and 25 min, respectively.

In order to investigate the performance of MIPs, the kinetics experiments and isothermal adsorption experiments of Fe3O4@MIPs and Fe3O4@NIPs were investigated according to the described method (Shen et al., 2024). The results indicate that the effective imprint sites are mainly distributed on the surface of Fe3O4@MIPs, and that the monolayer uniform adsorption and chemisorption are the main adsorption processes between effective imprint sites and AFB1 (Fig. S3 and Table S1- S2.).

The selectivity of Fe3O4@MIPs was evaluated by determining AFB1 and interferents (i.e. AFM1, AFG1, OTA and FB1). As shown in Fig. S4A, the adsorption capacity of Fe3O4@MIP for AFB1 is much higher than that of Fe3O4@NIPs. However, the adsorption capacity of Fe3O4@MIP for interferent was very low. The imprinting factor (IF) was also calculated (Hu et al., 2022), and the corresponding results were 2.82, 1.07, 1.23, 1.17, 1.06. In addition, the influence of interferents on the adsorption capacity of Fe3O4@MIP for AFB1 was investigated (Fi. S4B). All results indicate that Fe3O4@MIP has high selectivity. The reusability experiments were also performed. As shown in Fig. S4C, the adsorption capacity of Fe3O4@MIPs retains over 70 % of the original adsorption capacity after six cycles.

The SEM image and TEM image of CDs@MOF show the rhombic dodecahedral particles (Fig. S5). The morphology of the composite is consistent with that in the literature (Wang, Liu, et al., 2021). In addition, the high-resolution TEM of CDs@MOF shows that CDs are dispersed in the MOF nanocrystals with a size of about 2 nm (Fig. 1A). The XRD pattern of CDs@MOF (Fig. 1B) shows diffraction peaks consistent with those available in the literature, indicating the presence of CDs in MOF nanocrystals (Wang, Liu, et al., 2021). In addition, XPS was investigated to confirm the existence of CDs in MOF nanocrystals. Fig. 1C demonstrates the chemical composition of CDs@MOF, which includes Zn, O, N, and C. As shown in Fig. 1D, the C1s spectrum reveals peaks at 287.8, 286.0, 284.7, and 284.3 eV, corresponding to CO, CN, CC and CC bonds, respectively (Tripathy et al., 2022). The peak of CO at 287.8 eV could belong to the CDs in MOF nanocrystals (Wang, Liu, et al., 2021). Therefore, it is confirmed that CDs exist in MOF nanocrystals.

The peroxidase-like catalytic activity and fluorescence properties of CDs@MOF have been studied. Compared with the TMB–H2O2 system and TMB–CDs@MOF system (Fig. 1E), the addition of CDs@MOF deepened the color of the solution. The UV–vis absorption spectra confirm these results. Reaction kinetics of CDs@MOF in the TMB–H2O2 system was also investigated, and the results demonstrated the enzymatic activity of CDs@MOF (Fig. S6 and Table S3). The fluorescence properties of CDs@MOF can be seen in Fig. 1F. The excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior can be observed for CDs@MOF, and the maximum emission wavelength is 381 nm at an excitation of 280 nm.

The SEM morphology of the Fe3O4@MIP-AFB1-Apt-CDs@MOF sandwich structure is shown in Fig. 2A. The Apt-CDs@MOF are adsorbed on the surface of Fe3O4@MIP and form a sandwich structure. To further verify the formation of the sandwich structure, the results of zeta potential for Fe3O4, Fe3O4@MIP, Apt-CDs@MOF, and Fe3O4@MIP-AFB1-Apt-CDs@MOF are shown in Fig. 2B. Compared with Fe3O4, the zeta potential of Fe3O4@MIP shows a negative potential, suggesting successful imprinting (Li et al., 2023). In the presence of AFB1, Fe3O4@MIP and Apt-CDs@MOF complete their self-assembly and form a sandwich structure. A remarkable change in zeta potential is observed for Fe3O4@MIP-AFB1-Apt-CDs@MOF compared to Fe3O4@MIP and Apt-CDs@MOF alone, demonstrating the successful formation of sandwich structure.

The feasibility of the fluorescence/colorimetric strategy was verified. In the presence of AFB1, the color change of the suspension is not obvious (curve b in Fig. 2C) compared with Apt-CDs@MOF (curve a in Fig. 2C). This is due to the fact that Fe3O4@NIPs lacks imprinting sites, which hinders the formation of sandwich structures. Due to the large number of imprinting sites on the surface of Fe3O4@MIPs, the Fe3O4@MIP-AFB1-Apt-CDs@MOF sandwich structure is formed. When Fe3O4@MIPs-AFB1-Apt-CDs@MOF complexes are collected and removed by magnets, the Apt-CDs@MOF content in the suspension decreases, resulting in significant changes in the color of the suspension (curve c in Fig. 2C). The results of the fluorescence spectra are consistent with the above results (Fig. 2D). In the absence of AFB1, the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 2E) and fluorescence spectra (Fig. 2F) of different reaction systems show slightly change because no sandwich structure is formed. All in all, these results show that the fluorescence/colorimetric strategy is suitable for the detection of AFB1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the fluorescence/colorimetric strategy
ce:section.ce:para: To optimize the performance of the fluorescence/colorimetric strategy, several experimental parameters, were optimized (Fig. S7). When the ratio of Fe3O4@MIP to Apt-CDs@MOF, absorption time, pH for fluorescence detection and colorimetric detection are 1:5, 40 min, 5.0, and 4.0, respectively, the performance of the fluorescence/colorimetric strategy is the best.

In fluorescence mode, the fluorescence intensities of AFB1 in a range of 0.05–150 ng mL−1 are shown in Fig. 3A. Fig. 3B shows that the fluorescence intensity is inversely proportional to the logarithm of AFB1 concentration. The linear regression equation is Y = −1356.20 lg C + 4765.97 (R2 = 0.995). The limit of detection (LOD) and the limit of quantification (LOQ) for the fluorescence mode are 37 pg mL−1 (S/N = 3) and 125.5 pg mL−1 (S/N = 10), respectively. In colorimetric mode, Fig. 3C shows the variations of absorbance intensity with the AFB1 concentration from 0.1 to 100 ng mL−1. The absorbance intensities are inversely proportional to AFB1 concentration, with a linear equation of Y = −6.42 × 10−4C + 0.19 (R2 = 0.997). The LOD and LOQ for the colorimetric mode are 13.0 pg mL−1 (S/N = 3) and 43.9 pg mL−1 (S/N = 10), respectively. This fluorescence/colorimetric strategy was compared with other AFB1 determination methods (Table 1 ), and it confirms the great potential of this method in application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, repeatability, reproducibility and interference studies
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the fluorescence/colorimetric strategy was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 4A and B, AFB1 causes a stronger change in the fluorescence signal than others, indicating that the dual-mode sensor can specifically detect AFB1 in the presence of other mycotoxins. Fig. 4C shows that ions have little effect on the performance of the dual-mode sensor. The repeatability experiments were also performed. Fig. 4D shows that the fluorescence intensity after five cycles is about 135 % of the original adsorption capacity, and the color of the solution has changed slightly. Six dual-mode sensors were prepared to verify the reproducibility (Fig. 4E). The relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.92 % was calculated based on the fluorescence intensity, and the color of the solution did not change. As shown in Fig. 4F, the sensors were stored at room temperature. The response is 100.1 %, 107.4 %, 110.1 % and 110.3 % of the original fluorescence intensity after 4, 8, 12 and 16 days, respectively. It was found that the response of the sensor increased by more than 10 % after 12 days, indicating that the stability of the sensor was maintained for 12 days (Kuswandi et al., 2008). These results show that the dual-mode sensor has good repeatability, reproducibility and stability.

To illustrate the performance of the dual-mode sensor, the matrix interference is examined. The dual-mode sensor was used to detect peanut oil samples with different dilution ratios (dilution ratios: 0, 1, and 5) containing 15 ng mL− 1 AFB1. As shown in Fig. 4G and H, the signal changes are almost the same in different interferents. In this sandwich assay, the specific recognition ability of MIPs and aptamers excludes the vast majority of interfering substances (Geng et al., 2024). Subsequently, the Apt-CDs@MOF in suspension is used for fluorescence/colorimetric detection, which further excludes a very small number of interferents that could be adsorbed by the sandwich structure (Fe3O4@MIPs-AFB1-Apt-CDs@MOF).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring of AFB1 in edible oil samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practicality of the dual-mode sensor was further tested by analyzing AFB1 in edible oil samples. The peanut oil and olive oil samples were purchased from a local supermarket. The untreated samples were directly spiked with a standard solution AFB1 at different concentrations and left overnight. The results are in agreement with the ELISA results (Table S4). The recovery rates for the dual-mode sensor range from 89.4 % to 116.0 % and 98.4 % to 112.2 %, respectively.

In conclusion, a fluorescence/colorimetric sensing platform was developed based on the synergistic recognition of aptamer and MIP for the ultrasensitive and accurate detection of AFB1. The aptamer-MIP sandwich structure effectively excludes interfering substances, thereby ensuring the selective and accurate detection of AFB1 in edible oil. Based on the excellent peroxidase-like catalytic activity and fluorescence properties of CDs@MOF, a sensitive and accurate dual-mode sensor for the detection of AFB1 has been established, achieving LODs of 11.0 pg mL−1 and 13.0 pg mL−1 for the fluorescence and colorimetric channels, respectively. Furthermore, the dual-mode sensor utilizes independent yet correlated detection methods, which enhance the accuracy and reliability of the results. This dual-mode fluorescence/colorimetric strategy paves the way for the advancement of more accurate and efficient multimodal biosensors.

Xiaopeng Hu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Ke Wang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yufan Yang: Validation, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Baomiao Ding: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization. Chunqi Yu: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We greatly appreciate the support from Hubei Provincial Department of Education Science Research Program Young Talents Project (Q20231304).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.142387.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Bai et al., 2021

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Binding affinity-guided design of a highly sensitive noncompetitive immunoassay for small molecule detection
Food Chemistry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Bai, Y. Wang, Q. Li, L. Dou, M. Liu, S. Shao, J. Zhu, J. Shen, Z. Wang, K. Wen, W. Yu, Binding affinity-guided design of a highly sensitive noncompetitive immunoassay for small molecule detection, Food Chemistry 351 (2021) 129270.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Chi and Liu, 2023

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A fluorometric sandwich biosensor based on molecular imprinted polymer and aptamer modified CdTe/ZnS for detection of aflatoxin B1 in edible oil
LWT

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Chi, G. Liu, A fluorometric sandwich biosensor based on molecular imprinted polymer and aptamer modified CdTe/ZnS for detection of aflatoxin B1 in edible oil, LWT 180 (2023) 114726.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Cui et al., 2023
